title: The Rabies Vaccine Debuted Nearly 150 Years Ago Today
author: Bob Grant
text: Subscribe to skip adsAdvertisementHistoryThe Rabies Vaccine Debuted Nearly 150 Years Ago TodayPioneering microbiologist Luis Pasteur helped save the life of a dog-bitten boyBGBy Bob Grant5:00 PM CDT on July 6, 2026Share on FacebookShare on X (formerly Twitter)Share on RedditShare on EmailShare on BlueskyRabies is a viral disease that has plagued humanity for generations. Just last month, researchers in Canada published a case report about an 11-year-old boy who died of rabies about 5 weeks after he woke up with a bat on his face and nose in northern Ontario in 2024. A rabies vaccine could have saved his life, but just 150 years ago, such options weren’t available to people who experienced such brushes with rabid animals.Subscribe to skip adsFeatured VideoThat changed on July 6, 1885, when a 9-year-old French boy named Joseph Meister was the first person to be treated with an experimental rabies vaccine developed by none other than the father of modern microbiology, Louis Pasteur, in Paris.Meister was bitten 14 times by a rabid dog in Alsace two days earlier, and his mother Marie-Angélique had heard that the famed chemist was testing a rabies vaccine on dogs in Paris. She traveled to the metropolis and begged Pasteur to save her son. Initially the scientist balked at the idea, but the boy was near death and an influential Parisian physician named Jacques-Joseph Grancher convinced Pasteur and his colleagues to administer the vaccine to Meister.Subscribe to skip adsAdvertisement
